clinicalStem: A 58-year-old man with type 2 diabetes presents to the emergency department with a 2-day history of rapidly spreading erythema and severe pain in his right leg disproportionate to the visible skin changes. He appears toxic: temperature 39.8°C, HR 118 bpm, BP 90/60 mmHg, RR 24/min. Examination reveals wooden-hard induration, skin discolouration with patches of dark necrosis, and crepitus on palpation. WBC 22 × 10⁹/L, CRP 310 mg/L, creatinine 198 µmol/L. CT shows gas tracking along fascial planes. What is the single most important next step?
options: Urgent surgical debridement and fasciotomy
Start broad-spectrum IV antibiotics and reassess in 6 hours
Apply topical antiseptic dressings and admit for observation
Arrange urgent dermatology outpatient referral
Perform punch biopsy for histological confirmation before treatment
explanation: Necrotizing fasciitis is a life-threatening surgical emergency with mortality exceeding 70% if surgical intervention is delayed. The hallmarks here — pain disproportionate to appearance, crepitus, gas on CT, systemic sepsis — demand immediate operative debridement. Antibiotics alone cannot penetrate necrotic tissue and are adjunctive only.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — immediate surgical debridement is the only life-saving intervention; every hour's delay worsens mortality.
Antibiotics are necessary but wholly insufficient as the sole treatment; waiting 6 hours risks overwhelming septic shock and death.
Topical dressings have no role in necrotizing fasciitis; this approach would be fatal.
Outpatient referral is completely inappropriate for a patient in septic shock with necrotizing fasciitis.
Histological confirmation is not required and would cause fatal delay — diagnosis is clinical and radiological.
clinicalPearl: In necrotizing fasciitis, 'pain out of proportion to appearance' plus crepitus is a surgical emergency — operate first, culture later.
keyClue: Crepitus, gas on CT, pain disproportionate to visible changes, and systemic sepsis in a diabetic patient.
commonExamTrap: Selecting antibiotics as the primary intervention — they are essential but only adjunctive to urgent surgery.
missedConcept: Necrotizing fasciitis is primarily a surgical emergency; delay in debridement, not delay in antibiotics, is the key determinant of mortality.
redFlagReason: Necrotizing fasciitis with septic shock — delay in surgical debridement is rapidly fatal; this is a life-or-death clinical priority decision.

clinicalStem: A 19-year-old female student presents with severe nodulo-cystic acne that has failed two courses of oral antibiotics and topical retinoids over 18 months. She has significant scarring and is requesting isotretinoin. She is sexually active and currently uses no contraception. Before prescribing isotretinoin, which of the following is the single most essential pre-treatment requirement?
options: Confirm two negative pregnancy tests and enrol in a pregnancy prevention programme with mandatory effective contraception
Obtain baseline serum LFTs and full lipid profile only
Prescribe immediately since she is over 18 and has consented
Start a 3-month course of doxycycline first as isotretinoin is a last resort
Perform a skin biopsy to confirm cystic acne histologically before starting
explanation: Isotretinoin is a potent teratogen (FDA category X) causing major fetal malformations and miscarriage. Before prescribing, clinicians must confirm two negative pregnancy tests (first on days 2–3 of the cycle), enrol the patient in a structured pregnancy prevention programme, and ensure reliable dual contraception is in place. This is a mandatory regulatory safety requirement in most countries.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — two negative pregnancy tests and a formal pregnancy prevention programme with effective contraception are non-negotiable prerequisites in a woman of childbearing potential.
LFTs and lipid profile are important baseline investigations but are not the single most essential step before isotretinoin in a fertile woman — pregnancy prevention takes absolute priority.
Age and consent do not override the mandatory teratogenicity safeguards — prescribing without pregnancy precautions is clinically and legally inappropriate.
She has already failed two antibiotic courses; guidelines support isotretinoin at this stage. A third antibiotic course is not required and delays appropriate care.
Cystic acne is a clinical diagnosis — biopsy is not indicated and would delay treatment unnecessarily.
clinicalPearl: Isotretinoin must never be started without confirmed negative pregnancy tests and a formal pregnancy prevention programme in women of childbearing age — teratogenicity is absolute.
keyClue: Sexually active female with no contraception requesting isotretinoin.
commonExamTrap: Focusing on LFTs/lipids as the 'most essential' step and overlooking the paramount pregnancy prevention requirement.
missedConcept: Isotretinoin is teratogenic category X; pregnancy prevention is not optional — it is the primary safety gate before any prescription.

clinicalStem: A 7-year-old boy is brought to the GP by his mother with a 4-week history of patchy hair loss on the scalp. Examination reveals a 4 cm area of broken hair stubs, scaling, and mild erythema. Wood's lamp examination shows green fluorescence. Microscopy of hair scrapings confirms fungal hyphae. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Oral griseofulvin for 6–8 weeks plus selenium sulfide shampoo to reduce spore shedding
Topical clotrimazole cream applied to the affected area twice daily for 4 weeks
Topical ketoconazole shampoo alone for 8 weeks
Oral fluconazole single-dose therapy
Watchful waiting as tinea capitis is self-limiting in children
explanation: Tinea capitis requires systemic antifungal therapy because topical agents cannot penetrate the hair shaft. Griseofulvin is the first-line treatment for 6–8 weeks. Adjunctive selenium sulfide or ketoconazole shampoo reduces spore shedding and therefore decreases spread to contacts. Topical therapy alone is insufficient.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — oral griseofulvin is first-line; topical shampoo reduces transmission but cannot substitute for systemic treatment.
Topical antifungals alone cannot penetrate the hair follicle and will fail — systemic treatment is mandatory for tinea capitis.
Shampoo alone is insufficient monotherapy; it reduces spore shedding but does not eradicate the endothrix/ectothrix infection within the hair shaft.
A single dose of fluconazole is inadequate for tinea capitis, which requires weeks of therapy.
Tinea capitis does not resolve spontaneously in children and will progress, causing scarring alopecia if untreated.
clinicalPearl: Tinea capitis always requires systemic antifungal therapy — topical agents cannot reach the infection within the hair shaft.
keyClue: Patchy scalp hair loss with broken hair stubs, scaling, and green Wood's lamp fluorescence in a child.
commonExamTrap: Selecting topical clotrimazole — effective for other tinea infections but cannot penetrate the hair shaft in tinea capitis.
missedConcept: Topical therapy is insufficient for tinea capitis regardless of the antifungal chosen; the hair shaft barrier necessitates systemic treatment.

clinicalStem: A 34-year-old nurse develops sudden onset generalised urticaria, lip swelling, throat tightness, and audible stridor within 5 minutes of applying a latex glove at the start of her shift. Her BP is 80/50 mmHg, HR 130 bpm, SpO₂ 91% on room air. She is anxious and her voice is hoarse. What is the single most important immediate intervention?
options: IM adrenaline (epinephrine) 0.5 mg (1:1000) into the outer thigh
IV chlorphenamine 10 mg slow IV injection
IV hydrocortisone 200 mg
Nebulised salbutamol 5 mg
High-flow oxygen 15 L/min via non-rebreather mask only
explanation: This is anaphylaxis with airway compromise (stridor, hoarse voice) and haemodynamic shock — a life-threatening emergency. IM adrenaline 0.5 mg (1:1000) into the outer thigh is the first-line treatment and must be given immediately. It reverses bronchospasm, vasodilation, and laryngeal oedema. All other interventions (antihistamines, steroids, oxygen, salbutamol) are adjunctive and must not delay adrenaline.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — IM adrenaline is the only first-line life-saving drug in anaphylaxis; all else is secondary.
Chlorphenamine is an adjunct — it acts too slowly to reverse acute anaphylaxis and will not prevent airway loss or cardiac arrest.
Hydrocortisone takes hours to act and is a secondary adjunct — it has no role as an immediate first-line agent in anaphylaxis.
Salbutamol may help bronchospasm as an adjunct but does not reverse laryngeal oedema or shock and must not be given before adrenaline.
Oxygen is essential but does not reverse the anaphylactic process; without adrenaline the airway will close despite oxygen delivery.
clinicalPearl: In anaphylaxis, IM adrenaline is the only immediate life-saving drug — antihistamines and steroids are adjuncts and must never be given first.
keyClue: Stridor, hoarse voice, hypotension, and urticaria seconds after latex exposure — classic anaphylaxis with airway compromise.
commonExamTrap: Selecting chlorphenamine or hydrocortisone as the immediate treatment — both are secondary adjuncts and far too slow to save the airway.
missedConcept: Anaphylaxis is an ABCDE emergency; IM adrenaline takes priority over everything — the airway is closing.
redFlagReason: Anaphylaxis with stridor, hoarse voice, and haemodynamic shock — imminent airway loss; IM adrenaline must be given immediately or the patient may die.

clinicalStem: A 17-year-old girl presents with a 6-month history of facial acne. Examination shows numerous papules, pustules, and comedones over both cheeks and the forehead, with no nodules or cysts. She has tried topical benzoyl peroxide 5% for 3 months without adequate response. She has no contraindications to antibiotics. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Add oral doxycycline 100 mg daily combined with a topical retinoid
Start oral isotretinoin immediately
Switch to topical clindamycin monotherapy
Refer for intralesional steroid injections
Prescribe oral prednisolone 20 mg for 2 weeks to reduce inflammation
explanation: For moderate inflammatory acne that has failed topical monotherapy, guidelines recommend combined oral antibiotic (doxycycline or lymecycline) with a topical retinoid. The combination reduces antibiotic resistance and targets both comedogenesis and inflammation. Isotretinoin is reserved for severe/nodulo-cystic or treatment-resistant acne after trialling antibiotics.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — oral antibiotic plus topical retinoid is the evidence-based step-up for moderate acne failing topical monotherapy.
Isotretinoin is indicated for severe nodulo-cystic or truly refractory acne; moderate acne should first receive oral antibiotic therapy.
Topical clindamycin monotherapy promotes antibiotic resistance and is less effective than combination therapy; it is a step down, not up.
Intralesional steroids are used for individual cysts or nodules, not for widespread moderate acne as primary management.
Systemic corticosteroids have no role in routine acne management and would worsen acne with prolonged use.
clinicalPearl: Moderate acne failing topical therapy is treated with oral antibiotic plus topical retinoid combination — never oral antibiotic monotherapy, which promotes resistance.
keyClue: Moderate papulopustular acne, failed 3 months of topical benzoyl peroxide, no cysts or nodules.
commonExamTrap: Jumping to isotretinoin for moderate acne — it is reserved for severe/nodulo-cystic disease or genuine treatment failure after antibiotics.
missedConcept: The acne treatment ladder requires stepping up through oral antibiotics plus retinoid before escalating to isotretinoin for moderate disease.

clinicalStem: A 52-year-old woman with obesity presents with a 3-day history of increasing redness, warmth, and tenderness over her right lower leg. She has no recent trauma, no insect bite, and has not travelled recently. Temperature is 38.2°C, HR 96 bpm. Examination shows a sharply demarcated area of erythema, warmth, and oedema from the ankle to mid-calf. There is a small abrasion on the dorsum of the foot. D-dimer is mildly elevated at 0.6 mg/L. Doppler ultrasound of the right leg is negative for DVT. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Oral flucloxacillin 500 mg four times daily for 5–7 days with leg elevation and review in 48 hours
Therapeutic dose low-molecular-weight heparin as DVT cannot be excluded on clinical grounds
IV benzylpenicillin and admission given the elevated D-dimer
Topical fusidic acid cream to the affected area
Incision and drainage of the affected area
explanation: The clinical picture is consistent with non-purulent cellulitis caused by streptococci or staphylococci — warm, demarcated erythema with a clear portal of entry (abrasion), mild systemic features, and confirmed negative DVT ultrasound. Oral flucloxacillin is first-line for mild-moderate cellulitis in community settings, with leg elevation to reduce oedema and 48-hour review to ensure clinical improvement.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — negative Doppler rules out DVT; oral flucloxacillin with elevation and 48-hour reassessment is appropriate for mild-moderate cellulitis.
DVT has been excluded by negative Doppler ultrasound; anticoagulation is not indicated and carries bleeding risk.
IV antibiotics and admission are reserved for severe cellulitis (rapid spread, systemic sepsis, failure of oral treatment, immunocompromise) — this patient has mild-moderate disease.
Topical antibiotics do not penetrate deep enough to treat cellulitis — systemic therapy is required.
There is no fluctuance or abscess formation described; incision and drainage is not appropriate for non-purulent cellulitis.
clinicalPearl: A mildly elevated D-dimer alone does not diagnose DVT — Doppler ultrasound is definitive; confirmed negative DVT redirects management to cellulitis treatment.
keyClue: Sharply demarcated warm erythema with portal of entry (abrasion), mild fever, negative Doppler ultrasound for DVT.
commonExamTrap: Treating the mildly elevated D-dimer as confirmation of DVT — D-dimer is non-specific and must be interpreted alongside imaging.
missedConcept: Mildly elevated D-dimer is non-specific (raised in infection, inflammation, obesity) and does not mandate anticoagulation when Doppler is negative.

clinicalStem: A 64-year-old man with well-controlled type 2 diabetes presents with thickened, yellow-brown, crumbling toenails affecting three nails on his right foot over 2 years. Nail clippings sent for microscopy and culture confirm Trichophyton rubrum. He takes metformin and a statin. LFTs are normal. He asks about treatment options. Which treatment is most appropriate for achieving cure?
options: Oral terbinafine 250 mg daily for 12 weeks
Topical amorolfine nail lacquer for 4 weeks
Oral fluconazole 150 mg once weekly for 4 weeks
Nail avulsion alone without antifungal therapy
No treatment required as onychomycosis is cosmetic only in diabetics
explanation: Oral terbinafine for 12 weeks is the most effective treatment for dermatophyte onychomycosis (confirmed T. rubrum), achieving mycological cure rates of 70–80%. Terbinafine is fungicidal against dermatophytes. Topical therapy alone has inadequate penetration for established nail infection and poor cure rates. Diabetic patients are at higher risk of foot complications from untreated onychomycosis, so treatment is important.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — oral terbinafine 12 weeks is the evidence-based first-line treatment for dermatophyte toenail onychomycosis.
Topical amorolfine alone is insufficient for established onychomycosis affecting multiple nails — penetration is inadequate and cure rates are very low without systemic therapy.
Once-weekly fluconazole for 4 weeks is not the standard regimen for onychomycosis; treatment durations are significantly longer (weeks to months), and terbinafine is superior for dermatophytes.
Nail avulsion without antifungal therapy removes the infected nail but does not eradicate the fungal infection — regrowth will be re-infected.
Onychomycosis in diabetics is not merely cosmetic — thickened nails can cause pressure, wound formation, and secondary bacterial infection, all of which increase ulceration risk.
clinicalPearl: For dermatophyte onychomycosis, oral terbinafine 250 mg for 12 weeks is first-line — it is fungicidal against T. rubrum and superior to azoles for dermatophytes.
keyClue: Confirmed T. rubrum on culture, three toenails affected, 2-year duration — established onychomycosis requiring systemic therapy.
commonExamTrap: Selecting topical lacquer alone as sufficient treatment — it cannot achieve adequate nail penetration for established infection.
missedConcept: Topical antifungals are inadequate monotherapy for established onychomycosis; systemic terbinafine is required for acceptable cure rates.

clinicalStem: A 4-year-old girl with known atopic dermatitis presents with a widespread flare affecting the flexures of both elbows and knees, the face, and neck. Despite regular emollient use four times daily, she has persistent pruritus, sleep disturbance, and broken skin. Her mother has been reluctant to use any corticosteroids due to fears about skin thinning. The skin shows lichenification and excoriations. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Add a mild topical corticosteroid (hydrocortisone 1%) to inflamed areas, with continued emollients, and address parental concerns about steroid phobia
Prescribe a potent topical corticosteroid (betamethasone 0.1%) to all affected areas including the face
Start oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg for 5 days
Refer for immediate dermatology admission
Continue emollients alone for a further 4 weeks and then reassess
explanation: For a child with atopic dermatitis failing emollients, mild topical corticosteroids (hydrocortisone 1%) are the appropriate step-up, particularly on the face and flexures. Parental corticosteroid phobia is a major barrier to effective treatment; addressing misconceptions is a core part of management. Potent steroids are inappropriate for the face and in young children without specialist input.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — hydrocortisone 1% is safe and effective on the face and flexures in children; addressing steroid phobia is essential for treatment adherence.
Potent steroids (betamethasone 0.1%) should not be applied to the face or thin skin in young children due to significant atrophy and systemic absorption risk.
Systemic corticosteroids are not first-line for atopic dermatitis in children and risk rebound flare on withdrawal; topical therapy should be optimised first.
This flare does not meet criteria for emergency admission — appropriate primary care step-up should be attempted first.
Continuing emollients alone for 4 more weeks when the child has lichenification, sleep disturbance, and broken skin is inappropriate — active inflammation requires anti-inflammatory treatment.
clinicalPearl: Steroid phobia is the single biggest barrier to effective atopic dermatitis management — patient and parental education about the safety of appropriately potent topical steroids is as important as the prescription itself.
keyClue: Failed emollient monotherapy with lichenification and sleep disturbance; parental steroid phobia preventing escalation.
commonExamTrap: Using potent steroids on facial or flexural skin in a young child — mild potency steroids are correct for these sensitive areas.
missedConcept: Topical steroids must be matched to site and age — mild potency for face/flexures in children; steroid phobia education is a clinical intervention.

clinicalStem: A 28-year-old man presents with a 3-week history of intense itching, worse at night, affecting his hands, wrists, and trunk. His wife and 6-year-old daughter have similar symptoms. Examination reveals linear burrows between his fingers and on his wrists, with papules on the abdomen. Dermoscopy confirms Sarcoptes scabiei mites. What is the most appropriate management strategy?
options: Treat the patient and all household contacts simultaneously with permethrin 5% cream applied from neck to toes, left for 8–12 hours, repeated after 7 days
Treat only the patient with permethrin cream and advise household members to seek their own GP appointments
Prescribe oral ivermectin for the patient alone as it is more convenient
Apply topical hydrocortisone to relieve pruritus while awaiting spontaneous resolution
Treat only symptomatic household members and leave asymptomatic contacts untreated
explanation: Scabies eradication requires simultaneous treatment of all household and close contacts regardless of symptoms, because the incubation period means asymptomatic individuals can still be infested and act as re-infection sources. Permethrin 5% is first-line, applied from neck to toes (including face and scalp in young children), left for 8–12 hours, and repeated at 7 days to kill newly hatched mites. Treating index cases alone will lead to reinfestation.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — simultaneous treatment of all household contacts with permethrin 5%, two applications 7 days apart, is the evidence-based approach to prevent reinfestation.
Treating only the index case guarantees reinfestation from untreated household contacts — this strategy will fail.
Oral ivermectin is an alternative (especially in crusted scabies) but treating only the patient while leaving household contacts untreated will result in reinfestation.
Topical hydrocortisone does not treat the infestation — scabies requires a scabicide; pruritus persists for weeks after successful treatment and can be managed with antihistamines.
Asymptomatic contacts must be treated simultaneously — they are likely infested but not yet symptomatic and will reinfect treated individuals.
clinicalPearl: Treat ALL household contacts for scabies simultaneously — treating only symptomatic individuals leads to reinfestation within weeks.
keyClue: Nocturnal pruritus, linear burrows between fingers, multiple household members affected — classic scabies requiring contact treatment.
commonExamTrap: Treating only the symptomatic index case — reinfestation from asymptomatic contacts is the most common cause of 'treatment failure'.
missedConcept: Scabies treatment must cover all household contacts simultaneously, regardless of symptoms — the incubation period keeps many contacts asymptomatic but infectious.

clinicalStem: A 58-year-old woman with poorly controlled type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 86 mmol/mol) presents with a 6-week history of itching and burning in the skin folds beneath her breasts and in her groin. Examination reveals erythematous, moist, macerated plaques with satellite pustules beyond the main lesion border. Skin swabs confirm Candida albicans. Which of the following is the most appropriate management?
options: Topical clotrimazole 1% cream to affected areas twice daily, keep skin folds dry, and optimise glycaemic control
Systemic oral fluconazole 150 mg once weekly for 12 weeks as first-line
Topical hydrocortisone 1% cream alone to reduce inflammation
Prescribe antibiotics for secondary bacterial superinfection empirically
Apply zinc oxide barrier cream without antifungal treatment
explanation: Candidal intertrigo in a diabetic patient requires topical antifungal therapy (clotrimazole or miconazole) as first-line, alongside measures to reduce moisture in skin folds and — critically — optimisation of glycaemic control, which is the underlying predisposing factor. Candida thrives in hyperglycaemic environments, and recurrence will occur without HbA1c improvement.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — topical clotrimazole, skin fold hygiene, and glycaemic optimisation address the infection and the root predisposing cause.
Oral fluconazole is reserved for severe, recurrent, or systemic candidiasis — topical therapy is first-line for localised intertrigo.
Topical hydrocortisone alone without an antifungal will worsen candidal infection by promoting fungal growth in an immunosuppressed microenvironment.
Empirical antibiotics are not indicated — Candida is a fungal infection, not bacterial, and antibiotics would worsen the candidal overgrowth by disrupting bacterial competition.
Zinc oxide is a barrier cream but has no antifungal activity — candidiasis requires a scabicide.
clinicalPearl: Recurrent or extensive Candida intertrigo in a diabetic patient mandates glycaemic optimisation — antifungal treatment without addressing hyperglycaemia leads to relapse.
keyClue: Satellite pustules beyond the main lesion border in skin folds of a poorly controlled diabetic — classic Candida intertrigo.
commonExamTrap: Treating with topical steroid alone to reduce 'inflammation' — this worsens candidal infections by further immunosuppressing the local environment.
missedConcept: Satellite pustules are the hallmark of Candida intertrigo; hyperglycaemia is the key predisposing factor that must be addressed for durable cure.

clinicalStem: A 23-year-old woman presents with a 3-month history of itchy, vesicular rash on both earlobes and around her navel. The rash appears 24–48 hours after wearing metal earrings or her jeans belt buckle and resolves slowly over days when she removes them. Patch testing confirms nickel allergy. What is the most important initial management step?
options: Strict avoidance of nickel-containing metals and substitution with surgical steel, titanium, or plastic alternatives
Prescribe systemic antihistamines long-term to suppress the allergic reaction
Initiate nickel desensitisation immunotherapy
Apply topical antifungal cream given the vesicular morphology
Prescribe oral prednisolone 30 mg tapering course as definitive treatment
explanation: Allergic contact dermatitis (ACD) to nickel is a type IV delayed hypersensitivity reaction. The cornerstone of management is complete avoidance of the allergen — substituting nickel-containing metals with hypoallergenic alternatives (surgical steel, titanium, gold of high purity, or plastic). Antihistamines have limited benefit in type IV reactions, and desensitisation is not standard practice for contact allergens.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — allergen avoidance is the definitive treatment for ACD; no other intervention is as effective without addressing the cause.
Antihistamines (H1 blockers) have minimal effect on type IV (cell-mediated) hypersensitivity — they are more useful in type I (IgE-mediated) reactions.
Nickel desensitisation is not a standard or approved therapeutic option for contact allergy — avoidance is the only proven strategy.
Vesicles in ACD are due to spongiosis, not fungal infection — antifungal treatment is inappropriate here.
Oral prednisolone may suppress an acute severe flare but is not definitive treatment; without allergen avoidance, the rash will recur with every exposure.
clinicalPearl: Allergic contact dermatitis is a type IV hypersensitivity reaction — the only definitive treatment is identification and strict avoidance of the causative allergen.
keyClue: Reproducible vesicular rash at contact sites (earlobes, navel) 24–48 hours after metal exposure, confirmed on patch testing.
commonExamTrap: Prescribing antihistamines as the primary treatment — type IV reactions are T-cell mediated and largely antihistamine-resistant.
missedConcept: Type IV hypersensitivity is cell-mediated, not IgE-mediated — antihistamines are not effective; allergen avoidance is the only cure.

clinicalStem: A 16-year-old male started oral isotretinoin 3 weeks ago for moderate acne. He presents acutely with sudden severe worsening of facial and truncal acne, crops of haemorrhagic crusted ulcerating nodules, fever (38.9°C), arthralgia of both knees and ankles, and malaise. His CRP is 180 mg/L and WBC 17 × 10⁹/L. What is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Stop isotretinoin immediately and start oral prednisolone 0.5–1 mg/kg/day, with urgent dermatology referral
Double the isotretinoin dose to accelerate response as this is an expected flare
Add oral doxycycline 100 mg twice daily and continue isotretinoin
Admit for IV antibiotics as this represents superimposed bacterial infection
Continue isotretinoin at current dose and add topical corticosteroid to affected areas
explanation: This is acne fulminans — a rare, severe, systemic complication that can be triggered by isotretinoin. It presents with explosive ulcerating nodulo-cystic acne, fever, raised inflammatory markers, and arthralgia. The emergency management is to stop isotretinoin immediately and start systemic corticosteroids (prednisolone 0.5–1 mg/kg/day) to control the severe inflammatory cascade, followed by slow careful reintroduction of isotretinoin once inflammation is controlled under specialist supervision.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — acne fulminans requires immediate cessation of isotretinoin and systemic corticosteroids; continuing the drug worsens the inflammatory storm.
Doubling isotretinoin is contraindicated and dangerous — the drug is driving the inflammatory reaction; increasing the dose would be catastrophic.
Doxycycline cannot control acne fulminans; this is a systemic inflammatory condition requiring corticosteroids, and continuing isotretinoin perpetuates the reaction.
This is not primarily a bacterial infection — it is an acute inflammatory complication of isotretinoin; systemic antibiotics alone will not resolve it.
Continuing isotretinoin at any dose is contraindicated in established acne fulminans — the drug must be stopped.
clinicalPearl: Acne fulminans is an isotretinoin emergency — stop the drug immediately and start systemic corticosteroids; never continue or increase isotretinoin.
keyClue: Sudden ulcerating nodulo-cystic acne with fever, arthralgia, and raised CRP 3 weeks after starting isotretinoin.
commonExamTrap: Continuing or increasing isotretinoin thinking this is an 'initial flare' — acne fulminans is a distinct, dangerous complication requiring drug cessation.
missedConcept: Acne fulminans is a systemic inflammatory complication of isotretinoin — it is not an expected flare and requires immediate drug cessation and corticosteroids.
redFlagReason: Acne fulminans with systemic features (fever, arthralgia, CRP 180) — a genuine dermatological emergency requiring immediate isotretinoin cessation and corticosteroid therapy.
